summary: Patrick McKenzie provides a mid-February 2007 financial update for Bingo Card Creator, reporting $294 in gross sales from 11 units with roughly $195 in profit after expenses. Sales dipped around Valentine's Day, highlighting the need to anticipate holidays with special versions and ads. He details his cost structure including CD fulfillment, PayPal fees, AdWords, and hosting. Traffic has grown significantly, averaging 400 weekday visitors compared to 100 in November, and a blog post about problem customers went viral with 5,000 views reaching audiences far beyond his usual demographic.
key_points: Holiday periods like Valentine's Day cause sales to drop to zero, suggesting opportunity in creating holiday-specific versions and marketing
The business is profitable but modest: $294 gross on 11 sales with ~$195 profit after ~$108 in expenses including fulfillment, payment processing, and advertising
Website traffic has quadrupled from ~100 weekday visitors in November to ~400, with download peaks typically preceding sales peaks by about a week
A blog post about dealing with problem customers went viral (5,000 views), reaching audiences well beyond his core demographic of teachers and micro-ISV owners
money_quotes: I really need to start anticipating holidays and releasing special versions/ads for them, because otherwise my sales tend to decline to zero around them.
Wish me luck — typically, my sales peaks trail download peaks by about a week.

The one immediately below about dealing with problem customers has had about 5,000 views and, to judge by the email responses I am getting, has rocketed far out of my normal English-teacher-or-uISV-owner demographic.
I'm getting notes of commiseration from people as diverse as real estate agents and medical malpractice attourneys.
The other two are replacement CDs for two customers who got CDs from cd-fulfillment.com with a graphic that I had sized correctly, resulting in labels which are below my quality standards.
key_insight: Even a tiny software business can be consistently profitable when expenses are kept low, and content marketing through blogging can drive traffic growth that far exceeds paid advertising.
